title: Summarising Data with summarise() and group_by()
description: Compute summary statistics for groups using group_by() and summarise().

slides.title: summarise(): Reduce to Summary Statistics
slides.content:
`summarise()` (or `summarize()`) creates a new data frame with one row for each group (or overall if no grouping). It's used with aggregation functions like `mean()`, `sum()`, `sd()`, `n()`, etc.

```r
mtcars %>% summarise(avg_mpg = mean(mpg), total_hp = sum(hp))
```

slides.title: Basic summarise()
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>% summarise(
  mean_mpg = mean(mpg),
  sd_mpg = sd(mpg),
  n_cars = n()
)

slides.title: Grouped Operations with group_by()
slides.content:
`group_by()` changes the unit of analysis. Subsequent operations (like `summarise()`) are applied per group.

```r
mtcars %>%
  group_by(cyl) %>%
  summarise(avg_mpg = mean(mpg), n = n())
```

slides.title: group_by() + summarise()
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>%
  group_by(cyl, gear) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_mpg = mean(mpg),
    sd_mpg = sd(mpg),
    count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"  # to avoid a grouped result
  )

quizData.question: What does `n()` inside summarise() return?

options.text: The number of rows in the whole data frame
The number of rows in the current group

quizData.explanation: `n()` gives the group size when used with group_by().

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: After grouping, you can use `ungroup()` to remove grouping. Also, you can group by multiple variables (e.g., `group_by(cyl, gear)`).
